content:
First, by similarity of \(\triangle ABC\) and \(\triangle ACD\) (with the correspondence \(A\mapsto A,\;B\mapsto C,\;C\mapsto D\)) we get the scale factor
\[
\frac{AC}{AB}=\frac{6}{4}=\frac32.
\]
Hence
\[
CD = BC\cdot\frac32 = 5\cdot\frac32=\frac{15}2,
\qquad
AD = CA\cdot\frac32 = 6\cdot\frac32=9.
\]
Next we place
\[
A=(0,0),\quad C=(6,0).
\]
Then \(B\) lies on the circles \(|AB|=4\) and \(|BC|=5\).  One finds
\[
B=\Bigl(\tfrac94,\;\tfrac{5\sqrt7}4\Bigr).
\]
Similarly \(D\) lies on \(|AD|=9\) and \(|CD|=\tfrac{15}2\), and for convexity one takes
\[
D=\Bigl(\tfrac{81}{16},\;-\tfrac{45\sqrt7}{16}\Bigr).
\]
We seek \(P\) on the ray \(DA\) past \(A\), so
\[
P \;=\; A + t\,(A-D)\;=\;-t\,D,\qquad t>0,
\]
and we impose
\[
\angle BDC = \angle ACP.
\]
Translating both angles into dot‐products and equating cosines leads to a single quadratic in \(t\).  One finds the positive root
\[
t=\frac49,
\]
so
\[
P = -\frac49\,D
     =\Bigl(-\tfrac{9}{4},\;\tfrac{5\sqrt7}4\Bigr).
\]
Finally
\[
DP^2
=\Bigl\|\;P-D\Bigr\|^2
=\frac{43264}{256}
=169.
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{169}.
\]
